growth_form: Low herbaceous perennial, 5–20 cm tall, with a single fruit per stem.
habitat: Sphagnum bogs, tussock tundra, and wet peaty ground; abundant on the Mackenzie Delta.
distinguishing_features: Lobed maple-like leaves with five rounded lobes; solitary white flower; fruit ripens from red to amber-gold when ready.

photos.attribution: (c) vladimir_korotkov, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by vladimir_korotkov

growth_form: Low evergreen shrub forming dense mats; needle-like leaves; small black drupes.
habitat: Heath tundra, dry rocky ridges, and well-drained slopes throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Needle-like leaves resembling a tiny conifer; round, shiny black berries with a slight purple sheen at maturity.

photos.attribution: (c) Dmitry Kulakov, some rights reserved (CC BY), uploaded by Dmitry Kulakov

growth_form: Creeping evergreen dwarf shrub, 5–15 cm; small leathery oval leaves with rolled margins.
habitat: Heath tundra, dry to moist acidic soils; often grows alongside crowberry on rocky slopes.
distinguishing_features: Glossy oval evergreen leaves with dark dots beneath; tart bright-red berries that persist into spring under snow.

photos.attribution: (c) Andy Fyon, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Andy Fyon

growth_form: Low deciduous shrub, 10–60 cm tall, with multiple branched stems and round-tipped blue-green leaves.
habitat: Tussock tundra, dwarf shrub heath, and peaty soils throughout the ISR; common across the Mackenzie Delta.
distinguishing_features: Round-tipped, blue-green oval deciduous leaves with smooth (untoothed) margins; pale pink urn-shaped flowers; dusty-blue berries up to ~1 cm.

photos.attribution: (c) Ольга Курякова, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Ольга Курякова

growth_form: Creeping evergreen subshrub with thread-thin trailing stems, often half-hidden in sphagnum moss.
habitat: Sphagnum bogs and wet peat throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Tiny pointed evergreen leaves, whitish underneath; pink nodding flowers with strongly reflexed petals; small round red berries on long thread-like stems.

photos.attribution: (c) Matthieu Gauvain, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Matthieu Gauvain

growth_form: Prostrate deciduous dwarf shrub forming low mats; foliage turns brilliant red in autumn.
habitat: Heath tundra, dry rocky ridges, and exposed slopes throughout the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Net-veined obovate deciduous leaves that turn scarlet in fall; small urn-shaped white flowers; round berries ripening from green to glossy black.

photos.attribution: (c) Colin Chapman-Lam, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Low herbaceous perennial, 5–20 cm tall, with three-leaflet leaves.
habitat: Wet meadows, streambanks, and mossy ground; widely distributed across the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Trifoliate (three-leaflet) toothed leaves; solitary deep-pink five-petaled flower; small dark red aggregate berry resembling a tiny raspberry.

growth_form: Erect deciduous shrub 1–2 m tall, often along stream channels and gravelly disturbed ground.
habitat: Mackenzie Delta channels, gravelly riverbanks, and well-drained sites at the southern edge of the ISR.
distinguishing_features: Opposite oval leaves with silvery scaly undersides; tiny yellow-brown flowers; bitter translucent red-orange berries that froth when whipped.

photos.attribution: (c) bendingtree, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by bendingtree

growth_form: Small spreading shrub, 0.3–1 m tall, often sprawling along the ground.
habitat: Moist woods, streambanks, and shaded slopes along the Mackenzie Delta and southern ISR.
distinguishing_features: Maple-like (3- to 5-lobed) toothed leaves, glabrous; drooping racemes of small purplish flowers; translucent bright-red currants in early summer.

photos.attribution: (c) Vincent Laurie, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC)

growth_form: Prostrate dwarf shrub forming low mats, rarely exceeding 15 cm; woody stems creep along the ground.
habitat: Tundra throughout the ISR; widespread on dry to moist substrates including gravel ridges and disturbed ground.
distinguishing_features: Small oval leaves, shiny dark green above, paler below; catkins appear in early summer; dwarf creeping habit unmistakable on open tundra.

photos.attribution: (c) Mashuri Waite, some rights reserved (CC BY-NC), uploaded by Mashuri Waite
